summaryText: Guest Speaker Robert Madu opened with energetic praise and a warm welcome to Impact Church, sharing his excitement for what God is doing there. He preached from 2 Kings 5, focusing on the story of Naaman, a valiant soldier with leprosy. Naaman received a simple prescription for healing—to wash seven times in the Jordan River—but his reaction was to turn away in a rage. Pastor Robert used this moment to explore the universal emotion of anger, framing his message as 'The Cost of Going Off.' He explained that anger is a secondary emotion, often masking deeper issues like fear, pride, or unmet expectations. Naaman's rage stemmed from his wounded pride and status; he expected a grand, personal healing ceremony befitting his importance, not a humble act of obedience. The sermon highlighted that everyone has a 'spot'—an area of brokenness or sin—and that healing often comes through simple, humble obedience to God's instructions, not through our own efforts or status. Pastor Robert encouraged the congregation to put anger in its proper place, as Scripture instructs, and to not walk away from God when life doesn't meet our expectations, lest we miss the miracle He has for us. He concluded with an altar call, inviting those struggling with anger or needing salvation to surrender to Jesus.
mainPoints: Anger is a universal secondary emotion that must be understood and placed in its proper biblical context, not simply erupted or stuffed.
Healing and miracles often come through humble, simple obedience to God's instructions, not through our own status, efforts, or preconceived expectations.
Walking away from God in anger or pride when life doesn't meet our expectations can cause us to miss the very miracle our soul needs.

versesMentioned.text: Now Naaman was commander of the army of the king of Aram. He was a great man in the sight of his master and highly regarded, because through him the Lord had given victory to Aram. He was a valiant soldier, but he had leprosy.

Now bands of raiders from Aram had gone out and had taken captive a young girl from Israel, and she served Naaman’s wife. She said to her mistress, 'If only my master would see the prophet who is in Samaria! He would cure him of his leprosy.' Naaman went to his master and told him what the girl from Israel had said.

So Naaman went with his horses and chariots and stopped at the door of Elisha’s house. Elisha sent a messenger to say to him, 'Go, wash yourself seven times in the Jordan, and your flesh will be restored and you will be cleansed.' But Naaman went away angry and said, 'I thought that he would surely come out to me and stand and call on the name of the Lord his God, wave his hand over the spot and cure me of my leprosy. Are not Abana and Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? Couldn’t I wash in them and be cleansed?' So he turned and went off in a rage.

Naaman’s servants went to him and said, 'My father, if the prophet had told you to do some great thing, would you not have done it? How much more, then, when he tells you, ‘Wash and be cleansed’!' So he went down and dipped himself in the Jordan seven times, as the man of God had told him, and his flesh was restored and became clean like that of a young boy.
Whoever is patient has great understanding, but one who is quick-tempered displays folly.
'In your anger do not sin': Do not let the sun go down while you are still angry.

Monday.scripture: Ephesians 4:26 (NIV) - 'In your anger do not sin': Do not let the sun go down while you are still angry.
Monday.action: Identify one situation today that triggers frustration. Instead of reacting immediately, pause and acknowledge the feeling to God in a short, silent prayer.
Monday.prayer: Lord, help me to feel my emotions without letting them control me. Teach me to pause and bring my anger to you first. Amen.

Tuesday.scripture: 2 Kings 5:11-12 (NIV) - But Naaman went away angry and said, 'I thought that he would surely come out to me...'
Tuesday.action: Reflect on a recent disappointment. Write down what you 'thought' would happen and prayerfully surrender that expectation to God.
Tuesday.prayer: Father, my plans and expectations often fail. I surrender my 'I thought' to you today. Help me trust your process. Amen.

Wednesday.scripture: 2 Kings 5:13 (NIV) - 'My father, if the prophet had told you to do some great thing, would you not have done it? How much more, then, when he tells you, ‘Wash and be cleansed’!'
Wednesday.action: Is there a simple, clear instruction from God (like forgiving someone, starting a daily prayer time, serving humbly) you've been avoiding? Commit to doing that one simple thing today.
Wednesday.prayer: God, I often look for the grand gesture. Give me the humility to obey you in the simple, everyday things you ask of me. Amen.

Thursday.scripture: Proverbs 14:29 (NIV) - Whoever is patient has great understanding, but one who is quick-tempered displays folly.
Thursday.action: Practice patience in one specific interaction today (e.g., with a family member, coworker, or in traffic). Consciously choose a calm response.
Thursday.prayer: Holy Spirit, fill me with your patience. When I am tempted to be quick-tempered, remind me that understanding is a mark of wisdom. Amen.

Friday.scripture: 2 Kings 5:14 (NIV) - So he went down and dipped himself in the Jordan seven times, as the man of God had told him, and his flesh was restored...
Friday.action: Naaman had to dip seven times. Identify one area where you've been obedient but haven't seen results. Commit to one more step of faithful action without checking for immediate change.
Friday.prayer: Lord, I choose to trust your promise more than my perception. I will obey persistently, believing you are at work even when I can't see it. Amen.

Saturday.scripture: 2 Kings 5:1 (NIV) - He was a great man... highly regarded... a valiant soldier, but he had leprosy.
Saturday.action: Everyone has a 'spot.' In a moment of quiet honesty with God, name one area of brokenness or sin you've been trying to hide. Ask Him for healing.
Saturday.prayer: Jesus, I come to you just as I am. I confess my need for you in my hidden area of brokenness. I trust you to heal and restore me. Amen.
